title: Shallow Copy Surprise — Nested Objects
explanation: Spread creates shallow copy. Top-level primitives are safe. Nested objects share the same reference — mutating copy.b.c mutates original.b.c.
mentalModel: Spread = shallow copy. Primitives are safe, nested objects share the same reference

title: Object Keys Are Always Strings
explanation: Object keys are stringified. Both b and c become '[object Object]'. So a[b] and a[c] are the same key — second assignment overwrites first.
mentalModel: Object keys are stringified. Different objects become the SAME key: '[object Object]'

title: Assignment Copies the Reference
explanation: d = c copies the reference, not the object. Both point to the same memory. Mutating c.greeting also changes d.greeting.
mentalModel: Object assignment copies the reference (pointer). Both variables see the same data

title: Nullifying a Variable vs Mutating the Object
explanation: person = null redirects the variable, not the object. members[0] still references the original object. The object wasn't deleted.
mentalModel: Reassigning a variable doesn't affect other references. Only MUTATION is shared

title: Function Args — Objects by Reference, Primitives by Value
explanation: Objects passed by reference — member.name mutation affects original. primitives passed by value — birthYear reassignment doesn't affect outer variable.
mentalModel: Objects = reference (mutations shared). Primitives = value (copies are independent)

title: Object.freeze — Shallow Immutability
explanation: Object.freeze makes top-level properties read-only. box.x = 15 is silently ignored. But freeze is SHALLOW — nested objects can still be mutated.
mentalModel: Object.freeze = read-only at top level. Nested objects NOT frozen

title: Object Keys vs Set — Type Preservation
explanation: Object keys are always strings: obj[1] ↔ obj['1']. Set preserves types: has number 1, not string '1'.
mentalModel: Object keys = always strings. Set values = preserve original type

title: Spread Merge Objects — Order Matters
explanation: Spread creates copies of key/value pairs. The admin property is defined first, then user properties are spread in. No reference sharing — this is a NEW object.
mentalModel: Spread merge creates a new object with copied top-level properties

title: Duplicate Object Keys — Last Value Wins
code:
const obj = { a: 'one', b: 'two', a: 'three' };
console.log(obj);
explanation: When two keys have the same name, the last one overwrites. The key stays in its first position, but with the last-specified value.
mentalModel: Duplicate keys: last value wins, position stays at first occurrence

title: Object as Key — Dog/Cat Stringification
explanation: Both dog and cat objects stringify to '[object Object]'. So animals[dog] and animals[cat] are the same key. The cat assignment overwrites the dog assignment. animals[dog] returns the cat value.
mentalModel: All plain objects stringify to '[object Object]' — they all map to the same key

title: Class Instance — Reference Sharing via Assignment
explanation: counterTwo = counterOne copies the reference, not the object. Both point to the same Counter instance. counterTwo.increment() modifies the same object, so counterOne.count = 3.
mentalModel: Class instances are objects. Assignment copies the reference — both variables share the same instance

title: Primitive Value Copying — No Reference
explanation: Strings are primitives — they're copied by value. info.favoriteFood got the VALUE '🍕', not a reference to food[0]. Changing food[0] later doesn't affect info.
mentalModel: Primitives are copied by value. Changing the original doesn't affect the copy

title: Default Params — Spread Creates New Object Each Time
explanation: changeAge uses { ...person } as default — creates a NEW copy each call. Doesn't mutate original. changeAgeAndName uses person directly as default — mutates the original object.
mentalModel: Spread in default params = safe copy. Direct object reference in default = shared/mutable
